Luke chapter 22 forces us to witness a night of stunning contrasts. In the same room, at the same table, Jesus institutes the meal that will define His Church while announcing His own betrayal. He speaks of His body broken and blood poured out—words that carry the weight of substitution, of covenant fulfilled—even as one of His chosen men plots to deliver Him to death for thirty pieces of silver. Satan himself enters Judas, and yet Jesus does not wring His hands in surprise. He knows. He speaks of it plainly, even as the disciples argue about who among them is greatest. The scene is almost unbearable: infinite love preparing to die, surrounded by finite ambition and self-interest. And we must ask ourselves whether we stand with these disciples, or beside them—because their failure to understand who sits at their table mirrors our own constant drift toward thinking the kingdom works like the kingdoms of this world.

The weight of this chapter rests on what Jesus does in Gethsemane. We see Him not as the victorious King speaking calmly of His fate, but as a Man in genuine anguish, sweating drops of blood, begging His Father if there is any other way. This is not weakness or lack of resolve. This is the horror of what He is about to bear—the full reality of human sin placed upon the one Person who never committed it, the separation from His Father that sin demands. And still He submits. "Yet not My will, but Yours be done." In these words, the entire purpose of His coming finds its expression. He does not come to be served but to serve, not to save His own life but to give it. The disciples cannot stay awake to watch with Him for even one hour. Their exhaustion from sorrow—that haunting phrase—reveals how little they understand of what is actually happening, even as it unfolds before them.

What follows is betrayal met with healing. Judas approaches with a kiss, the ancient sign of friendship and honor, now weaponized into treachery. And when Peter's sword slices off the servant's ear, Jesus does the unthinkable—He heals the very man who has come to arrest Him. He touches the wound and restores it. This is not the response of someone forced into submission; it is the deliberate choice of a Man whose entire mission is to absorb violence and return mercy. Peter will deny knowing Jesus three times before the night ends, but his denials pale against the reality that Jesus knows Him, has prayed for Him, and will restore Him. The hour of darkness has come, but it belongs only to darkness because Jesus allows it. He could have called down legions of angels. Instead, He walks toward the cross as the only path by which sinners can be reconciled to God. That is what this chapter stakes everything upon—not the triumph of His enemies, but the triumph of His love.
